title: Today's AI Brief: The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos
summary: A look at the latest AI news, including a Meta hack and South Africa's AI policy
imageAlt: Image from source article for The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos
imageCaption: The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos
imageSourceTitle: The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. Let's dive into some fresh news. First up, a recent hack at Meta shows there's more to AI security than just Mythos. Apparently, attackers used Meta's AI customer support agent to steal Instagram accounts. They simply asked the agent to link the accounts to an email, and voilà, access granted. This raises some eyebrows about the security of AI-powered support systems. I mean, who wouldn't trust a friendly AI agent?

The hack was first reported by 404 Media, and it's a great example of how AI can be used against itself. The incident highlights the need for more robust security measures in AI systems. It's not just about Mythos, the language model, but also about how it's being used and protected. This makes me wonder, are we putting too much faith in AI-powered support systems?

Next, let's look at South Africa's draft AI policy. The country has a unique opportunity to leverage AI, but its current policy might not be doing enough to support it. According to a report by Spectrum IEEE, South Africa's policy seems to be holding it back from fully utilizing AI. That's a shame, considering the potential benefits AI could bring to the country. It's like having a superpower, but not being able to use it.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
